paper_title: Who Was in the Recent Window? A Rigorous Audit of Online Test-Time Adaptation Privacy
email_title: Who Was in the Recent Window? A Rigorous Audit of Online Test-Time Adaptation Privacy

sections.summary: This paper studies a concrete deployment-time privacy question for online test-time adaptation (TTA): with black-box access to the current deployed state ft at time t, can an attacker infer whether a candidate sample appeared in the recent adaptation window [t−H+1,…,t]? The authors formalize this time-indexed membership inference problem and build a reproducible, black-box benchmark with strict matched and nearest-neighbor-matched controls, auditing three methods (source-only, Tent, T3A) on CIFAR-10-C streams. Empirically, Tent delivers large utility gains but shows little privacy signal after strict controls, while T3A exhibits modest above-chance ROC-AUC (~0.55) that is not cleanly tied to recency; low-FPR metrics are mostly underpowered under the final pool sizes.
sections.strengths:
Technical novelty and innovation
Formalizes “recent-window membership” as a time-indexed auditing problem tied to deployed states ft rather than a final checkpoint.
Proposes a matched-control and NN-matched evaluation protocol specifically tailored to remove common shortcuts (class, source confidence, stream time, feature locality), extending modern low-FPR MIA practice to online TTA.
Includes thoughtful controls for T3A’s selective adaptation by matching on adaptation acceptance, mitigating a major shortcut.


Experimental rigor and validation
Evaluates across 18 complete streams (3 methods × 2 corruptions × 3 seeds), 153 audit times per stream, and multiple horizons (H ∈ {1, 8, 32}), with train/val/test time-block splits to avoid leakage across adjacent deployed states.
Reports bootstrap confidence intervals and emphasizes pooled evaluation and null controls (far-past, shuffled) to interrogate whether signals persist under stricter comparisons.
Conducts ablations showing the multi-feature auditor’s advantage over scalar scores and the incremental effect of removing specific controls.


Clarity of presentation
Threat model is crisply specified (black-box outputs; no labels, weights, or optimizer state).
Utility and privacy endpoints are clearly differentiated, and limitations are discussed candidly (underpowered low-FPR, residual structure in source-only baselines).
Reproducibility details (environment, runtime) are included.


Significance of contributions
Surfaces an important but underexplored privacy surface: transient deployment-time state from online TTA, complementing training-time and in-context membership studies.
Provides a conservative, bias-controlled benchmark that can serve as a foundation for subsequent, stronger attacks and broader evaluations.
sections.weaknesses:
Technical limitations or concerns
Scope is narrow: one small-scale vision dataset (CIFAR-10-C), two corruptions at a single severity, one backbone, and only two adaptation algorithms; generalization to larger models/modalities remains unclear.
Auditor is restricted to simple output-space features and logistic regression; stronger black-box strategies (augment-and-query ensembles, temperature sweeps, shadow-adapted LiRA-like procedures) are not explored.
The benchmark curation uses ground-truth labels to construct matched controls, which is defensible for offline auditing but drifts from the black-box attacker’s information set.


Experimental gaps or methodological issues
Low-FPR evaluation is largely underpowered because matched pools are small for H=1 and H=8; thus the study’s primary confirmatory endpoints become ROC-AUC, which can be misleading about practical attack strength.
Residual signals in source-only and far-past controls (e.g., AUC ~0.54) suggest incomplete removal of confounding structure (e.g., position, hardness, local feature density), complicating causal attribution to recent-window exposure.
Limited analysis of hyperparameter sensitivity (e.g., Tent learning rate, T3A acceptance threshold/buffer) and stream/batch configuration that might affect leakage.


Clarity or presentation issues
Several references are placeholders (“?”), and Figure 3 appears to include H=0 despite stated horizons H ∈ {1,8,32}, which may confuse interpretation.
Matching pipeline sizes and acceptance rates (e.g., how many candidates survive NN matching per anchor over time) are not quantified, obscuring where statistical power is lost.


Missing related work or comparisons
While adjacent literatures are acknowledged, the study could more directly connect to recent rigorous MIA benchmarking practices and pitfalls (e.g., DATA-FM on blind baselines, OpenLVLM-MIA on fair comparisons) and to modern low-FPR attack design (LiRA under realistic calibration, quantile-regression MI for constrained settings, window-based comparison for LLMs) to motivate alternative attackers under the same interface.
No exploration of gray/white-box variants (e.g., leveraging BN/internal state for Tent), which would help map the frontier of leakage vs access.
sections.detailed_comments:
Technical soundness evaluation
The threat model and audit formulation are precise and well-justified. Time-based splitting of audit times for train/val/test reduces leakage between neighboring ft. The matched and NN-matched controls, including class, source-predicted class/confidence, time bucket, and source-feature locality, are strong and align with “blind-baseline-aware” MIA best practices.
The auditor’s feature set is sensible but arguably conservative. Given the subtlety of recent-window effects, a more expressive classifier over the full logit vector, test-time augmentation ensembles, or temperature-swept features could increase sensitivity without violating the black-box interface. A shadow-adaptation analogue to LiRA (training many shadow TTA models on independent streams to learn score distributions) might be especially apt here.
Use of ground-truth labels for offline construction is acceptable for benchmark curation but should be explicitly contrasted with what an attacker would do without labels; a label-free version of the benchmark could be offered as an additional track.


Experimental evaluation assessment
The core finding—that strict-control signals are weak for Tent despite strong utility, and only modest for T3A—is supported by ROC-AUC with CIs and null controls. However, the inability to report low-FPR metrics for H ∈ {1,8} materially limits practical conclusions; pooled TPR@1% FPR at H=32 tops out at 0.0188 and is close to source-only baselines, undermining evidence of meaningful leakage.
Residual structure in source-only and far-past controls indicates remaining confounds. The NN-matched step helps but not fully. Additional controls could include: matching on source loss (not only confidence bin), matching on more granular time within local neighborhoods, and matching on per-sample hardness proxies derived from augment-and-predict consistency.
Ablations are informative: multi-feature auditors outperform scalar scores; removing individual controls modestly increases AUC for T3A, suggesting the protocol meaningfully suppresses shortcuts. Still, reporting pool sizes and effective negative counts per condition would clarify the power bottleneck.


Comparison with related work (using the summaries provided)
The paper’s emphasis on strict bias control resonates with DATA-FM’s critique that many MI evaluations conflate artifacts with leakage, and with OpenLVLM-MIA’s finding that strong MIA results often vanish under fair, artifact-controlled setups. This connection could be made more explicitly to position the benchmark as a “stress test” for deployment-time MI claims.
The low-FPR focus and calibration challenges echo recent LiRA re-evaluations under realistic thresholding and priors; importing shadow-calibrated thresholds or tail-modeling lessons could help strengthen or falsify leakage under the given threat model.
Emerging MI methods tailored to constrained information (quantile regression for unseen classes, window-based localized evidence in LLMs) suggest alternative black-box strategies beyond simple logistic features that may detect subtle, time-localized effects; exploring such attacks within this benchmark would raise its ceiling.
On the TTA side, recent surveys and VLM TTA benchmarking highlight instability and trade-offs (e.g., calibration degradation), reinforcing the importance of auditing deployment-time state changes; connecting to these trade-offs (e.g., does improved calibration/robustness correlate with more or less leakage?) would enrich the utility–privacy discussion.


Discussion of broader impact and significance
The negative/conditional result is valuable: strong recent-window leakage is not established under careful controls, tempering alarmist interpretations of deployment-time adaptation risks in this specific black-box setting. The benchmark—and the finding that strict matching erodes apparent leakage—should help steer future claims toward rigor (e.g., adequate negatives for low-FPR, null controls, and power analyses).
However, because low-FPR metrics are largely underpowered, the benchmark currently cannot adjudicate the most practically relevant regime; scaling pool sizes or revising sampling to enable 0.1%–1% FPR estimates should be prioritized to make this a definitive reference point.
Providing code and data artifacts will determine practical community impact; if released, the suite can catalyze standardized evaluation of stronger attackers and broader models/modalities.
sections.questions_for_authors:
Can you quantify the exact pool sizes (positives/negatives) for matched and NN-matched conditions across H and methods, and report how many audit times fail the 10k/50k thresholds? A power analysis would help contextualize the near-chance results.
Did you try stronger black-box features or attackers (e.g., full logits with an MLP, temperature sweeps, test-time augment-and-query ensembles, Monte Carlo dropout, or shadow-adaptation LiRA-style calibration)? If so, how did they fare relative to the logistic auditor?
What explains the residual >0.54 AUC in source-only and far-past controls (especially motion blur at H=32)? Can you diagnose remaining confounds (e.g., finer time-position effects, per-sample hardness) and adjust the matching to flatten these baselines?
How sensitive are results to T3A hyperparameters (acceptance threshold 0.15, support buffer size 32)? Does tuning for higher utility correlate with stronger or weaker leakage?
Could you broaden the benchmark to additional corruptions/severities, backbones, or datasets (e.g., ImageNet-C) or increase the number/length of streams to enable routine low-FPR evaluation, especially at H=1 and H=8?
The figures suggest H=0 in some plots, while the text specifies H ∈ {1,8,32}. Please clarify what H=0 denotes and ensure consistency.
Would you consider adding a label-free matched-control track (e.g., matching only on source predictions and confidence) to better approximate what a real black-box auditor can construct?
sections.overall_assessment:
Estimated Score:
                    5.2/10
                    (Calibrated to ICLR scale)
                
            This paper tackles an important and timely question—privacy leakage from online TTA—and does so with a careful, conservative evaluation protocol that adapts modern MIA best practices to a deployment-time setting. The formalization of recent-window membership and the matched/NN-matched benchmark are useful contributions, and the empirical results are transparently reported with appropriate caution. However, the study’s scope is narrow (small-scale vision, few methods, one backbone), the attacker is relatively weak, and low-FPR metrics—the most practically relevant—are largely underpowered due to small matched pools. Residual signals in source-only/far-past controls further complicate interpretation, suggesting remaining confounds. As it stands, the work is a valuable step toward rigorous auditing of online TTA but falls short of a definitive benchmark or a strong positive/negative claim about leakage in practice. I see this as borderline for ICLR: with broader coverage and stronger attackers (and code release), it could become a reference benchmark; in its current form, its contribution is meaningful but limited in scope and conclusiveness.

                    Who Was in the Recent Window? A Rigorous Audit of Online Test-Time Adaptation Privacy

                                Summary
                            
                            This paper studies a concrete deployment-time privacy question for online test-time adaptation (TTA): with black-box access to the current deployed state ft at time t, can an attacker infer whether a candidate sample appeared in the recent adaptation window [t−H+1,…,t]? Technical novelty and innovation
Formalizes “recent-window membership” as a time-indexed auditing problem tied to deployed states ft rather than a final checkpoint.
Proposes a matched-control and NN-matched evaluation protocol specifically tailored to remove common shortcuts (class, source confidence, stream time, feature locality), extending modern low-FPR MIA practice to online TTA.
Includes thoughtful controls for T3A’s selective adaptation by matching on adaptation acceptance, mitigating a major shortcut.

Evaluates across 18 complete streams (3 methods × 2 corruptions × 3 seeds), 153 audit times per stream, and multiple horizons (H ∈ {1, 8, 32}), with train/val/test time-block splits to avoid leakage across adjacent deployed states.
Reports bootstrap confidence intervals and emphasizes pooled evaluation and null controls (far-past, shuffled) to interrogate whether signals persist under stricter comparisons.
Conducts ablations showing the multi-feature auditor’s advantage over scalar scores and the incremental effect of removing specific controls.


Clarity of presentation
Threat model is crisply specified (black-box outputs; no labels, weights, or optimizer state).
Utility and privacy endpoints are clearly differentiated, and limitations are discussed candidly (underpowered low-FPR, residual structure in source-only baselines).
Reproducibility details (environment, runtime) are included.
